title: The friend we only call when we're in trouble
summary: Argue and reflect on the nature of friendships where contact happens mainly during a crisis, and consider whether this kind of relationship is still valuable.
topic: The friend we only call when we're in trouble
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing whether a friendship that only becomes active during a crisis is still a real friendship. Describe a real or imagined example of such a relationship. Use present perfect for observations, at least one concession with 'although' or 'while', a second-conditional sentence, and at least two modal verbs.
hintStarters: Most of us have at least one person we only contact when something has gone badly wrong…
I'd argue that a friendship which survives long silences but responds instantly to a cry for help is not a lesser friendship — it's…
If we measured friendship only by how often two people speak, we would miss…

hintVocab.meaningVi: bạn chỉ đến khi thuận lợi

hintVocab.phrase: to pick up where you left off
hintVocab.meaningVi: tiếp tục từ chỗ đã dừng lại
sự hỗ trợ vô điều kiện
coi ai đó là hiển nhiên
thử thách một mối quan hệ
nỗ lực từ cả hai phía
bỏ tất cả mọi thứ lại
sự sẵn sàng về mặt cảm xúc

sampleText:
There is a woman I met during my first year at university in Warsaw who I have not spoken to in three years. Last autumn, when I lost my job and did not know how to tell my family, I sent her a message at midnight. She called back within twenty minutes. We spoke for two hours. By the end of the call, I could breathe again. Then we went back to silence, and neither of us seemed to find this strange.

Although a friendship like this might look like neglect in either direction, I think it is built on something quite solid: the knowledge that certain people will always show up when it actually matters. The everyday check-ins, the small talk, the shared photographs — these are pleasant, but they are not the test. The test is whether someone will drop everything for you at midnight, and some friendships pass that test even without regular contact. If we measured the strength of a relationship only by how often two people speak, we would overlook the kind of loyalty that does not need to announce itself. A friend in Dublin told me recently that the people she trusts most are the ones she can go months without seeing and still feel completely safe with.

However, there is a version of this kind of friendship that crosses a line. It becomes a one-sided arrangement when one person consistently reaches out only in crisis and never gives back even small, ordinary attention in calmer times. The person who is always available may begin to feel used, and that is a reasonable feeling. While I believe that quality matters more than frequency in friendship, I also think that some minimum of mutual effort is necessary to keep the relationship alive on both ends. Checking in occasionally — a message, a voice note, a brief question about how someone's life is going — costs almost nothing and signals that the person exists to you beyond their worst moments. A friendship that only activates under pressure can still be real, but it should not be entirely one-directional, or it will eventually wear out the person who keeps showing up.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: did not know how to tell my family
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: không biết cách nói với gia đình
gọi lại trong vòng hai mươi phút
được xây dựng trên nền tảng khá vững chắc

sampleAnnotations.phrase: show up when it actually matters
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: xuất hiện khi điều đó thực sự quan trọng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The test is whether someone will drop everything
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Thử thách là liệu ai đó có bỏ tất cả lại

sampleAnnotations.phrase: does not need to announce itself
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: không cần phải tự tuyên bố

sampleAnnotations.phrase: consistently reaches out only in crisis
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: liên tục chỉ liên lạc trong lúc khủng hoảng
chất lượng quan trọng hơn tần suất
nỗ lực từ cả hai phía
chỉ kích hoạt dưới áp lực

sampleAnnotations.phrase: wear out the person who keeps showing up
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: làm kiệt sức người luôn xuất hiện

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for a lasting connection
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'There is a woman I met during my first year at university in Warsaw who I have not spoken to in three years' kết hợp quá khứ đơn ('met') cho thời điểm cụ thể trong quá khứ và hiện tại hoàn thành ('have not spoken') cho khoảng thời gian kéo dài đến hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'There is a woman I met during my first year at university in Warsaw who I have not spoken to in three years' combines simple past ('met') for a specific past moment and present perfect ('have not spoken') for a period that extends to now.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for a general argument
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If we measured the strength of a relationship only by how often two people speak, we would overlook the kind of loyalty that does not need to announce itself' dùng quá khứ đơn + would + bare infinitive để lập luận một điểm giả định — chúng ta không nên đo lường tình bạn theo cách đó.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If we measured the strength of a relationship only by how often two people speak, we would overlook the kind of loyalty that does not need to announce itself' uses past simple + would + bare infinitive to argue a hypothetical point — implying we should not measure friendship this way.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một người phụ nữ tôi quen trong năm đầu đại học ở Warsaw mà tôi đã không nói chuyện được ba năm. Mùa thu năm ngoái, khi tôi mất việc và không biết cách nói với gia đình, tôi đã nhắn tin cho cô ấy lúc nửa đêm. Cô ấy gọi lại trong vòng hai mươi phút. Chúng tôi nói chuyện hai tiếng. Đến cuối cuộc gọi, tôi có thể thở lại được. Sau đó chúng tôi quay lại im lặng, và không ai trong chúng tôi có vẻ thấy điều này lạ.

Mặc dù một tình bạn như thế này có thể trông như sự lơ là từ cả hai phía, tôi nghĩ nó được xây dựng trên nền tảng khá vững chắc: sự hiểu biết rằng một số người sẽ luôn xuất hiện khi điều đó thực sự quan trọng. Những cuộc kiểm tra hàng ngày, những cuộc trò chuyện nhỏ, những bức ảnh được chia sẻ — những điều này dễ chịu, nhưng chúng không phải là thử thách. Thử thách là liệu ai đó có bỏ tất cả lại vì bạn lúc nửa đêm không, và một số tình bạn vượt qua thử thách đó ngay cả khi không có liên lạc thường xuyên. Nếu chúng ta đo lường sức mạnh của một mối quan hệ chỉ bằng tần suất hai người nói chuyện, chúng ta sẽ bỏ qua loại lòng trung thành không cần phải tự tuyên bố. Một người bạn ở Dublin gần đây nói với tôi rằng những người cô ấy tin tưởng nhất là những người cô ấy có thể không gặp nhiều tháng và vẫn cảm thấy hoàn toàn an toàn.

Tuy nhiên, có một phiên bản của loại tình bạn này vượt qua ranh giới. Nó trở thành một thỏa thuận một chiều khi một người liên tục chỉ liên lạc trong lúc khủng hoảng và không bao giờ đáp lại ngay cả sự chú ý nhỏ, bình thường trong những thời điểm bình lặng hơn. Người luôn sẵn sàng có thể bắt đầu cảm thấy bị lợi dụng, và đó là một cảm xúc hợp lý. Trong khi tôi tin rằng chất lượng quan trọng hơn tần suất trong tình bạn, tôi cũng nghĩ rằng một số nỗ lực tối thiểu từ cả hai phía là cần thiết để duy trì mối quan hệ còn sống ở cả hai đầu. Kiểm tra thỉnh thoảng — một tin nhắn, một ghi âm giọng nói, một câu hỏi ngắn về cuộc sống của ai đó đang thế nào — không tốn gì nhiều và báo hiệu rằng người đó tồn tại với bạn ngoài những khoảnh khắc tồi tệ nhất của họ. Một tình bạn chỉ kích hoạt dưới áp lực vẫn có thể là thật, nhưng nó không nên hoàn toàn một chiều, nếu không nó cuối cùng sẽ làm kiệt sức người luôn xuất hiện.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect to describe a gap in contact?
options: I did not speak to her in three years.
I have not spoken to her since three years.
I have not spoken to her in three years.
I haven't been spoken to her for three years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect + 'in + time period' (negative) or 'for + duration' describes a gap from the past to now.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'have not + past participle + in/for' for an unbroken gap up to the present.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly to make an argument?
options: If we measure friendship only by frequency, we would overlook a lot.
If we measured friendship only by frequency, we will overlook a lot.
If we would measure friendship only by frequency, we would overlook a lot.
If we measured friendship only by frequency, we would overlook a lot.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause + 'would + bare infinitive' in the result clause. No 'would' in the if-clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Never use 'would' in the if-clause of a second conditional.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'while' to introduce a concession correctly?
options: While I believe that quality matters more than frequency, I also think some effort is necessary.
While I believe that quality matters more than frequency, but I also think some effort is necessary.
While, I believe that quality matters more than frequency, I also think some effort is necessary.
I believe that quality matters more than frequency while, I also think some effort is necessary.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'While + clause, main clause' — no 'but' and no comma immediately after 'while'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'While' as a concession works like 'although' — the main clause follows without 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which modal verb best expresses an obligation or necessity in an argument?
options: Some minimum of mutual effort might be necessary to keep the relationship alive.
Some minimum of mutual effort is necessary to keep the relationship alive.
Some minimum of mutual effort can be necessary to keep the relationship alive.
Some minimum of mutual effort should keep the relationship alive.
mcQuestions.explanation: Here, 'is necessary' states a firm position without hedging. The other options express doubt ('might'), ability ('can'), or advice ('should') rather than a reasoned conclusion.
mcQuestions.hint: When stating a conclusion in an argument, use 'is necessary' rather than a hedging modal.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly describes someone who is present only when things are going well?
options: a sunny-day friend
a good-times friend
a fair-weather friend
a bright-weather friend
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Fair-weather friend' is the fixed idiom for someone who is only present when life is easy.
mcQuestions.hint: This is a fixed two-word compound — only one weather word collocates correctly.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'reach out' to describe making contact?
options: One person consistently reaches out only during the crisis.
One person consistently is reaching to out only in crisis.
One person consistently reaches to out only in crisis.
One person consistently reaches out only in crisis.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Reach out' is a phrasal verb meaning to contact someone. It does not take 'to' between the verb and the particle.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Reach out' is intransitive in this sense — no 'to' between 'reach' and 'out'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses correct punctuation when 'however' appears mid-sentence?
options: However, there is a version of this kind of friendship that crosses a line.
However there is a version of this kind of friendship that crosses a line.
There is however a version of, this kind of friendship that crosses a line.
There is, however a version of this kind of friendship that crosses a line.
mcQuestions.explanation: When 'however' opens a sentence as a contrast marker, it is followed by a comma.
mcQuestions.hint: A sentence-opening 'however' needs a comma directly after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause without unnecessary repetition?
options: the people she trusts most are the ones that she they can go months without seeing
the people she trusts most are the ones she can go months without seeing
the people she trusts most are the ones who she can go months without to see
the people who she most trusts are the ones she they can go months without seeing
mcQuestions.explanation: After the relative pronoun, do not repeat the subject with 'they' or misuse 'to' after 'without'.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid double subjects in relative clauses: 'she can go' is correct, not 'she they can go'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the gerund correctly after a preposition?
options: A friendship that only activates under pressure it can still be real.
A friendship which it only activates under pressure can still be real.
A friendship that only activates under pressure can still be real.
A friendship only activating under pressure, it can still be real.
mcQuestions.explanation: The relative clause 'that only activates under pressure' modifies 'friendship' cleanly. No pronoun repetition is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: The relative clause subject is already defined by 'that' — don't add 'it'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly uses 'take for granted'?
options: Taking someone as granted means not appreciating them until they are gone.
Taking someone to granted means not appreciating them until they are gone.
Taking for granted someone means not appreciating them until they are gone.
Taking someone for granted means not appreciating them until they are gone.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Take someone for granted' is a fixed phrase. The preposition is 'for', and the object goes between 'take' and 'for'.
mcQuestions.hint: The preposition in this phrase is 'for', not 'as' or 'to'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'pick up where you left off'?
options: They were able to pick up where they had left off, talking for hours.
They were able to pick up where they had left, talking for hours.
They were able to pick up from where they left off from, talking for hours.
They were able to pick up where they left, off talking for hours.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Pick up where someone left off' is the full fixed phrase — 'off' must stay attached to 'left'.
mcQuestions.hint: The full idiom is 'pick up where [someone] left off' — don't separate or move 'off'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a difference between a friendship that only surfaces in a crisis and one that is simply low-maintenance? At what point does low-maintenance become neglect?
